read.dek: After the ceasefire announcement, Jiang refuses to read the moment as peace. The ceasefire is narrative control. Iran, Trump, and Netanyahu all need to look forced or righteous, but the deeper engine is religious: each actor believes he is carrying a divine mission that turns danger into energy and war into salvation.
read.thesis: The episode moves from current-events analysis into political theology. The announced ceasefire does not end the war; it lets every side define the moral script before the next stage. Jiang's model is messianic calling: a chosen leader receives divine energy, becomes fearless, converts persecution into proof of election, and imagines death as martyrdom. That model then explains three missions. The Ayatollah wants to lure the great Satan into Iran and kill it. Trump wants an unwinnable Iran war to destroy the American empire and restore the American Republic. Netanyahu wants the Kingdom of David restored, and Christian Zionists inside the American/global order want the same catastrophe because it prepares their prophecy.

opening.heading: Core Reading
opening.text: Peace is not the first thing to see. The ceasefire is a fight over the story of the war: who wants peace, who is forced, who is the aggressor, and who is acting for God. In this reading, the decisive actors are not simply calculating interest. They are charged by messianic calling. A mission from God gives them energy, makes bullets feel secondary, makes persecution feel like proof, and turns war into the path by which each one saves a people or remakes the world.

Trump needs the American people to believe he wants peace but is being driven by globalists, the deep state, neocons, and the American empire. Both sides can posture as peacemakers because Israel can be made to carry the visible role of aggressor.
